result:
On the third floor of a building with no address, there is an office that receives every letter never sent. It exists wherever a thought pauses at the rim of a mouth and retreats, wherever a hand hovers over a mailbox and turns away. The hinge that keeps it there is simple stubbornness and complicated mercy. Inside, the light is patient, sifting through high windows and resting on stacks of envelopes sorted by the temperature of their hesitation. Some are warm with urgency that cooled to ash. Some are cold as bridges in winter. The dust is polite. The air smells faintly of stamps, even though none have ever been licked. I know this because I work there, though no one hired me, and I have no paycheck except time.

Each morning I unlock the cabinets with keys that do not jingle. The labels are not names but angles: Toward Courage, Away From Harm, Under The Weather, Over One's Head. Within, the unsent accumulate like snowdrifts that never melt. I do not read them all. That would be a theft. I brush my fingers along the edges, listening to the different paper speak. Coarse apologies rasp. Smooth stationery whispers like lake water. Newsprint clucks. On the days when storms gather in other people's throats, we feel it here, a low pressure of might-have-beens. The windows ache the way old scars do before rain. My work is simple: I measure, I alphabetize by heartbeat, I file by the weight of regret.

Sometimes an envelope starts to hum. It means the person who wrote it is walking past a postbox and feeling the gravity of nearly. It means the thought has warmed again, a coal breathed upon. I have learned not to interfere. The rule, written nowhere, is that I cannot send what could have been sent, because history is a tangled hair and I only own a comb. But sometimes I set the humming envelope on the windowsill, and the light does something to it, a soft unraveling, a suggestion of road. A few have vanished this way, which I do not take personally. When they go, the room gains an ounce of brightness, and somewhere, perhaps, a shoulder releases.

There are categories so specific they make me smile. Letters written in bathrooms at weddings. Letters drafted upon learning a favorite tree was cut down. Letters to teachers twenty years later to say the grain of praise still lodges beneath the tongue. There is a drawer for poems addressed to rain that were never brave enough to be called poems. There is a metal box for the monster questions: Will you forgive me, Could I have this back, Do you love me. Sometimes the boxes rattle. We tighten their lids. We do not read. It is not piety. It is a respect for the architecture of decision, which is sometimes elegant and sometimes merely a door that will not open.

Once, a letter arrived with my name on it. The handwriting was my own, but steadier. It had the brittle quiet of paper that crossed a winter. I kept it unopened for a week, tucked under the scale we use to weigh the rumors. I made tea and set the cup beside it each morning, as if warmth could teach it to behave. On the eighth day I slit the envelope. Inside was a single sentence, written twice. It said: Stop concluding for everyone else. Stop concluding for everyone else. The second time it was underlined. I folded it back into its envelope, then into another, then another, like a child making a worry charm, small as a seed.

We keep a bowl of stamps no country recognizes. People who find us by accident sometimes ask for one as a souvenir. I let them choose, though I warn that they stick to nothing outside this room. A boy once pressed a blue sun to his forehead and said it felt like a doorbell. An old woman sorted through them by taste, touching each to her tongue like the ghosts of strange fruit. Another visitor looked around and said, This is just a room full of cowardice. He was right, but only the way a weather report is right: there will be rain, and also people singing under umbrellas, and also gutters learning how to speak.

The building has no address, but deliveries arrive all the same. We receive crates of unsent postcards, their pictures bleaching into an equal sky: the same beach, the same mountain, the same cathedral blessing the same pigeons. A child somewhere writes, Wish you were here, then erases you and writes anyone. A man writes nothing at all and slides the blank card between bills, a galleon among ledgers, then loses his nerve and keeps it. I stack their unsaid cities into skyline after skyline. When the afternoon is quiet, I imagine the addresses would have learned one another, a correspondence of destinations talking among themselves: Do you ever tire of being longed for.

At closing I sweep. Paper flits like pale moths and comes to rest again. I turn the lamp off from the cord because the switch has grown superstitious. I count the steps to the stairwell: thirteen, then the one I always forget. On the landing there is a window that frames a sliver of lake. I stand there longer than necessary and practice not deciding. The water knows how to hold every unmade ripple at once. It turns the sky into something it can keep. When I finally walk down, the city feels like an envelope facing its addressee, the whole night gathered under a name, the stamp of the moon damp on its corner, waiting, waiting, then held.

In the morning it will begin again: breath pausing, ink stammering, hands hovering above a mouth, a mailbox, a beating door. I will unlock what never opened, sort the weather, listen for the hum, and promise to send nothing, except maybe myself, someday, soon.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
